questions.question: What is an FBAR?
questions.answer: An FBAR (Foreign Bank Account Report) is sent directly to the Department of Treasury to report foreign bank account balances. If you had more than $10,000 USD worth of foreign currency in one account, or a total of $10,000 USD spread across multiple foreign bank accounts, you need to file an FBAR.

questions.question: When are expat taxes due?
questions.answer: Taxes are due in mid-April each year, but filers who are outside of the US and its territories on April 15th get an automatic extension until June 15. It is generally understood that expat taxes are due in June — however, if you owe taxes, those payments are due in April, and interest will start to accrue after the standard April deadline.

questions.question: Who needs to file US taxes?
questions.answer: All Americans, green card holders, and residents — those with income, earned or unearned, in the US or from a US institution — are required to file taxes no matter where they live. Non-resident aliens who have streams of income from the US must also file.

questions.question: I'm missing salary history and can't contact my previous employer. What can I do?
questions.answer: This is common in the expat world — expats move countries, jobs, cities, and houses more than most. If you don't have payslips, you can use bank records, an employment contract, national year-end tax documents, or a combination thereof. If we're relying on your memory alone, you must disclose that — and that's perfectly fine. There is a proper way to handle everything; all I need is full disclosure.

questions.question: Can you work with me if I live far away or can't meet in person?
questions.answer: Absolutely! Many of our clients are distance clients. We work with people across the Korean peninsula, China, Japan, Taiwan, Hong Kong, Thailand, Australia, Germany, India, Turkey, and beyond. Many clients continue to use our services even after repatriating to the US — that first year back can be tricky, and we're happy to help. No matter where you are, we are here for you.

questions.question: What is a tax preparer responsible for?
questions.answer: There are two types of preparers — both are qualified to calculate, file, and sign income tax returns on behalf of individuals and businesses. "Enrolled" preparers can also represent the taxpayer during IRS examinations. "Unenrolled" preparers do not represent clients in front of the IRS. All preparers are required to file the facts as they know them to be.

questions.question: I'm married to a non-American. How does this affect my taxes?
questions.answer: Does your partner have a green card? Does your partner have any income coming from the US? If you answered "no" to these questions, it is unlikely that your partner's income will affect your tax return. The 2018 tax reforms changed how non-resident alien (NRA) spouses can be identified on a return. If you wish to list your NRA spouse on your return, let us know — we have excellent work-arounds for these situations.

questions.question: I'm ashamed I haven't filed in so long. Are you going to judge me?
questions.answer: Oh, if I had a dollar for every time I heard this... I am not here to judge you; I am here to help you sort through the facts and offer the best solutions. Your situation might be messy — it might be a mini-mess or a mega-mess — but I have likely seen messier. Expat messes are my wheelhouse. And if you have a galactic-level mess, I know people. I got you.

questions.question: How does your process work?
questions.answer: Once you decide to file with us, you'll receive a Google Form via email to collect the information we need. After returning the form, you pay the filing fee (we accept Korean bank transfer, American transfers, and PayPal). Once both the form and fee are received, we get to work — thoroughly, accurately, and promptly.

questions.question: Can you do Korean taxes too?
questions.answer: Staying current on Korean taxes is critical, especially since it's now required for visa renewal. While we are not legally permitted to prepare Korean taxes ourselves, we have a network of amazing Korean tax preparers and are happy to make a referral. Visit our Korean Taxes page for details.
